comment_text.sn9.4:2.1: The idiom viya me khāyati (“seems to me”) is used for suppositions, so it must refer to the presumed dissatisfaction of the mendicants, rather than the disappointment of the deity.
comment_text.sn9.4:4.4: A Brahmanical renunciate is said to wander “without fire or abode” (anagniraniketa, Manusmṛti 6.25, 6.43).

root_text.sn9.4:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ sambahulā bhikkhū kosalesu viharanti aññatarasmiṁ vanasaṇḍe.
root_text.sn9.4:1.2: Atha kho te bhikkhū vassaṁvuṭṭhā temāsaccayena cārikaṁ pakkamiṁsu.
root_text.sn9.4:1.3: Atha kho yā tasmiṁ vanasaṇḍe adhivatthā devatā te bhikkhū apassantī paridevamānā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn9.4:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, aññatarā devatā taṁ devataṁ gāthāya paccabhāsi:

translation_text.sn9.4:1.1: At one time several mendicants were staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove.
translation_text.sn9.4:1.2: Then after completing the three months of the rainy season residence, those mendicants set out wandering.
translation_text.sn9.4:1.3: Not seeing those mendicants, the deity haunting that forest cried. And on that occasion they spoke this verse:
translation_text.sn9.4:2.3: They were so learned, such brilliant speakers!
translation_text.sn9.4:2.4: Where have these disciples of Gotama gone?”
translation_text.sn9.4:3.1: When they had spoken, another deity replied with this verse:
translation_text.sn9.4:4.1: “They’ve gone to Magadha, they’ve gone to Kosala,
translation_text.sn9.4:4.2: and some are in the Vajjian lands.
translation_text.sn9.4:4.3: Like deer that wander free of ties,
translation_text.sn9.4:4.4: the mendicants live with no abode.”

variant_text.sn9.4:1.2: vassaṁvuṭṭhā → vassaṁ vutthā (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
